BRIDGE suggested Daejeon because of its central location (50 minutes to Seoul by KTX), affordable cost of living, and the presence of KAIST and other universities that create a younger, more international atmosphere in the Yuseong area.

I was matched with a hagwon in Seo-gu that teaches elementary through middle school students. The salary is 2,300,000 KRW per month with a rent-free furnished apartment.

### Life in Daejeon

Daejeon has become my ideal base camp for experiencing Korea.

**My travel record in 6 months:**
- Seoul (8 times — concerts, museums, nightlife)
- Busan (3 times — beaches, BIFF, Jagalchi Market)
- Gyeongju (twice — the ancient capital, incredible temples)
- Jeonju (once — bibimbap heaven, hanok village)
- Sokcho/Seoraksan (once — best hiking I have ever done)
- DMZ Tour (once — surreal and unforgettable)

All of these trips started from Daejeon Station, usually for under 30,000 KRW by KTX or express bus. Being in the center of the country makes everywhere accessible.

**Daily life in Daejeon:**
- My apartment is a 5-minute walk from work — no commute
- I spend about 700,000 KRW per month total (food, transport, phone, entertainment)
- Seongsimdang bakery is a Daejeon institution — their Twigim Soboro bread is legendary
- Yuseong Hot Springs area has free foot baths and a relaxing vibe
- Gyejoksan barefoot trail is my go-to weekend morning walk
- The Daejeon expat group on social media organizes events almost weekly

### My Teaching Experience

I teach 6 classes per day, each 40 minutes, Monday through Friday. My students are great — curious, energetic, and often hilarious. The curriculum is structured, but my director encourages me to add my own style. I use a lot of games and competitive activities, which works well with the Korean students' naturally competitive spirit.

My psychology background has actually been surprisingly useful in the classroom. Understanding child development and behavioral patterns helps me manage classes and connect with students who might be struggling or acting out.

### Financial Snapshot

| Item | Monthly |
|------|--------|
| Salary | 2,300,000 KRW |
| Rent | 0 (provided) |
| Food | 350,000 KRW |
| Transportation | 80,000 KRW |
| Phone | 35,000 KRW |
| Entertainment/Travel | 200,000 KRW |
| **Total savings** | **~1,600,000 KRW (~$1,200 USD)** |

After 6 months, I have saved enough to pay off a significant chunk of my student loans while still traveling extensively.

### Advice for Americans

- Start the FBI check **the day you decide to come to Korea** — it is the bottleneck
- Use a document service for the State Department apostille unless you are near D.C.
- Daejeon is the best-kept secret in the Korean ESL world — central, affordable, livable
- BRIDGE was honest about every school they recommended. When I asked about a position they had concerns about, they told me straight up instead of pushing it
- Bring your favorite snacks from home — Korean convenience stores are amazing, but sometimes you just want Flamin' Hot Cheetos

I am renewing my contract next month. The plan is two years total, enough to clear my loans and build savings. BRIDGE made getting here easy. Staying was my own choice — and an easy one.

posts.title: Rachel P. — New Zealand to Jeju
posts.body:
# Rachel P. — New Zealand to Jeju Island

**Background**: 26, from Auckland. Bachelor's in Communications, 120-hour TEFL certificate. No prior teaching experience.

## Why I Chose Korea
I'd been working in marketing for two years and felt stuck. A friend who taught in Seoul kept posting these amazing travel photos and I thought — why not? I found BRIDGE through a Reddit thread and sent in my application on a whim.

## The Process
The BRIDGE team was incredibly responsive. Within a week of my application, I had a phone consultation where they asked about my preferences. I said I wanted somewhere with nature and a smaller community feel — they immediately suggested Jeju.

My interview with the school was smooth. The director was friendly, asked standard questions, and seemed genuinely interested in what I could bring to the table. BRIDGE prepped me beforehand so I knew what to expect.

Documents took about 6 weeks total (NZ police check + apostille + degree verification). BRIDGE walked me through every step and kept a shared checklist so nothing fell through the cracks.

## Life on Jeju
I teach at a small academy in Jeju City. My students are mostly elementary-aged and absolutely hilarious. The Korean co-teacher is wonderful and has become one of my closest friends here.

Jeju is stunning — I hike Hallasan on weekends, swim at hidden beaches, and the cost of living is lower than Auckland. My apartment overlooks tangerine orchards. I sometimes have to pinch myself.

## The Honest Parts
- I miss my family, especially during Korean holidays when everyone goes to their hometowns
- The language barrier was frustrating for the first two months
- My school expected more admin work than I anticipated
- But the support from BRIDGE made every challenge manageable

## Would I Recommend BRIDGE?
Absolutely. They weren't just a recruiter — they felt like a support system. Even months after I arrived, I could message them with questions and get a response within hours.

**Time in Korea**: 14 months and counting. Already renewed my contract.

posts.title: Chris M. — USA to Daejeon
posts.body:
# Chris M. — USA (Texas) to Daejeon

**Background**: 31, from Houston. Bachelor's in History, CELTA certified. 2 years teaching experience in Thailand.

## Switching from Thailand to Korea
I loved Thailand but wanted better salary and savings potential. Korea was the obvious choice — higher pay, free housing, and a strong education system. After some bad experiences with other recruiters, I found BRIDGE through ESL Cafe forums.

## Why BRIDGE Was Different
Most recruiters I'd dealt with before felt like used car salesmen — pushing any job to get their commission. BRIDGE actually listened. When I said I wanted a hagwon with maximum 25 teaching hours and a school that wouldn't pile on admin work, they found exactly that.

They were also transparent about what to expect. "This school is great but the director can be strict about punctuality" — that kind of honest heads-up is rare in this industry.

## Daejeon — The Underrated City
Seoul gets all the hype, but Daejeon is incredible for quality of life. It's smaller, cheaper, less crowded, and still has KTX access to Seoul in under an hour. The food is amazing, the people are friendly, and I have a spacious apartment that would cost triple in Gangnam.

## My School
I teach middle school students at an English academy. Classes are small (6-8 students), the curriculum is well-organized, and my Korean co-teacher handles all parent communication. I work 9:30 AM to 6:30 PM with genuine breaks.

## Finances
On a 2.7M salary with free housing:
- Monthly expenses: about 800K won
- Monthly savings: about 1.5M won (roughly $1,100 USD)
- Severance after year 1: 2.7M won bonus
- This is life-changing for someone who was living paycheck to paycheck in the US.

## Advice for Others
1. Don't limit yourself to Seoul — smaller cities offer better value
2. Trust your recruiter but verify everything in the contract
3. Bring a good attitude and be flexible
4. Korea rewards teachers who stay and build relationships

**Time in Korea**: 2 years. Planning to stay at least one more.

posts.title: Photo Tips — 사진이 합격을 좌우합니다
posts.body:
## Photo Tips for ESL Teacher Applications
## 사진이 합격을 좌우합니다

Your photo is the first thing a recruiter sees. In Korea's competitive ESL market, a professional photo can make the difference between getting an interview and being overlooked.

당신의 사진은 채용 담당자가 가장 먼저 보는 것입니다. 한국의 경쟁적인 ESL 시장에서 전문적인 사진은 면접 기회를 얻는 데 결정적인 역할을 합니다.

### Do's

- **Dress professionally** — business casual at minimum (collared shirt, blouse)
- 비즈니스 캐주얼 이상의 복장을 착용하세요 (카라 셔츠, 블라우스)
- **Smile naturally** — Korean employers appreciate a warm, approachable expression
- 자연스럽게 미소 지으세요 — 한국 고용주들은 따뜻하고 친근한 표정을 선호합니다
- **Use good lighting** — natural light facing you, no harsh shadows
- 좋은 조명을 사용하세요 — 자연광이 얼굴을 향하도록, 강한 그림자 없이
- **Plain background** — white or light-colored wall
- 깔끔한 배경을 사용하세요 — 흰색 또는 밝은 색 벽
- **High resolution** — at least 600x800 pixels, clear and sharp
- 고해상도 사진을 사용하세요 — 최소 600x800 픽셀, 선명하게

### Don'ts

- **No selfies** — they look unprofessional regardless of quality
- 셀카는 피하세요 — 품질과 관계없이 비전문적으로 보입니다
- **No sunglasses or hats** — your face must be clearly visible
- 선글라스나 모자를 쓰지 마세요
- **No group photos** — even if cropped, the quality suffers
- 단체 사진에서 잘라낸 사진은 피하세요
- **No filters** — Instagram or Snapchat filters are never appropriate
- 필터를 사용하지 마세요
- **No party or vacation photos** — keep it professional
- 파티나 휴가 사진은 절대 사용하지 마세요

### Pro Tips

**Budget option:** Ask a friend to take your photo against a white wall with a smartphone camera. Use portrait mode if available. Natural daylight from a window works great.

**저예산 팁:** 친구에게 흰 벽 앞에서 스마트폰으로 사진을 찍어달라고 부탁하세요. 가능하면 인물 모드를 사용하고, 창문에서 들어오는 자연광을 활용하세요.

**Investment option:** A professional headshot from a portrait photographer costs $50-100 and is worth every penny. Many photographers offer "LinkedIn headshot" packages that work perfectly.

**투자 팁:** 전문 사진작가의 프로필 사진은 5~10만 원 정도이며, 그만한 가치가 충분히 있습니다.

posts.title: TEFL Certification — 자격증의 힘
posts.body:
## TEFL Certification — The Power of a Teaching Certificate
## 자격증의 힘

While not always legally required, a **TEFL/TESOL/CELTA** certificate significantly boosts your chances of landing a better position in Korea.

법적으로 항상 필수는 아니지만, **TEFL/TESOL/CELTA** 자격증은 한국에서 더 좋은 포지션을 얻을 확률을 크게 높여줍니다.

### Why Get Certified?

1. **Competitive advantage** — Hundreds of applicants may apply for the same position. A TEFL certificate helps you stand out.
2. **Higher salary** — Many schools offer 100,000-200,000 KRW more per month to certified teachers.
3. **Immigration preference** — Korean immigration increasingly values TEFL certification, especially for E-2 visa applications.
4. **Better preparation** — You will actually learn valuable classroom techniques.

### 왜 자격증을 취득해야 하나요?

1. **경쟁 우위** — 같은 포지션에 수백 명이 지원할 수 있습니다. TEFL 자격증이 차별화 요소가 됩니다.
2. **높은 급여** — 많은 학원이 자격증 소지 강사에게 월 10~20만 원 추가 급여를 제공합니다.
3. **비자 심사 우대** — 출입국관리사무소에서 TEFL 자격증을 긍정적으로 평가합니다.
4. **실질적인 준비** — 실제 교실에서 쓸 수 있는 유용한 교수법을 배우게 됩니다.

### Which Certificate Should You Get?

| Certificate | Hours | Cost | Recognition |
|-------------|-------|------|-------------|
| **CELTA** (Cambridge) | 120+ (in-person) | $1,500-2,500 | Gold standard, highest recognition |
| **Trinity CertTESOL** | 130+ (in-person) | $1,500-2,000 | Equivalent to CELTA |
| **Online TEFL (accredited)** | 120+ | $200-500 | Widely accepted in Korea |
| **Weekend/Short TEFL** | 20-40 | $100-200 | Minimal value, not recommended |

### Recommendations

- **Minimum 120 hours** — Anything less is not taken seriously by Korean employers or immigration
- 최소 120시간 이상의 과정을 이수하세요
- **Accredited programs** — Look for courses accredited by recognized bodies
- 인증된 프로그램을 선택하세요
- **In-person practicum** — If possible, choose a course that includes observed teaching practice
- 가능하면 실습(교생 실습)이 포함된 과정을 선택하세요

### Already in Korea?

Several institutions in Korea offer in-person TEFL courses, which can be completed on weekends while you are already teaching. Ask BRIDGE for current recommendations.

posts.title: Interview Tips — 인터뷰 꿀팁
posts.body:
## Interview Tips for ESL Teaching Positions
## 인터뷰 꿀팁

Most ESL interviews in Korea are conducted via **video call** (Zoom, Google Meet, or KakaoTalk). Here is how to make a great impression.

대부분의 한국 ESL 인터뷰는 **화상 통화**로 진행됩니다. 좋은 인상을 남기는 방법을 안내합니다.

### Before the Interview

- **Research the school** — Check their website, Naver reviews, and social media. Know their student age range and teaching style.
- 학교를 사전 조사하세요 — 웹사이트, 네이버 후기, SNS를 확인하고, 학생 연령대와 교육 방식을 파악하세요.
- **Test your equipment** — Camera, microphone, and internet connection. Use a wired connection if possible.
- 장비를 사전 테스트하세요 — 카메라, 마이크, 인터넷 연결 상태를 확인하세요.
- **Prepare your background** — Clean, uncluttered space. Good lighting on your face.
- 배경을 정리하세요 — 깔끔한 공간, 얼굴에 좋은 조명.

### Common Interview Questions

1. **"Tell me about yourself"** — Keep it to 2 minutes. Focus on your teaching experience, education, and why you want to teach in Korea.
2. **"Why Korea?"** — Show genuine interest in Korean culture, not just "I want to travel."
3. **"Describe your teaching style"** — Give specific examples of activities you have used.
4. **"How would you handle a difficult student?"** — Show patience and adaptability.
5. **"What age group do you prefer?"** — Be honest but flexible.

### Korean-Employer-Specific Tips

- **Be punctual** — Log in 5 minutes early. In Korean business culture, being on time means being early.
- 5분 일찍 접속하세요. 한국 비즈니스 문화에서 정시는 일찍 도착하는 것을 의미합니다.
- **Dress professionally** — Even for a video call, wear a collared shirt or blouse.
- 화상 통화라도 단정한 복장을 갖추세요.
- **Show enthusiasm** — Korean employers value energy and a positive attitude, especially for younger learners.
- 열정을 보여주세요 — 한국 고용주들은 에너지와 긍정적인 태도를 중시합니다.
- **Ask questions** — About curriculum, class sizes, support materials, and the local area.
- 질문을 준비하세요 — 교육과정, 반 규모, 교재, 지역 환경 등.

### Questions to Ask the Employer

- What curriculum or textbooks do you use?
- What is the average class size?
- What is the typical daily schedule?
- Is housing provided? Can I see photos?
- What support is available for new teachers?

### Red Flags to Watch For

- Reluctance to provide a written contract before arrival
- Vague answers about working hours or salary
- Pressure to make an immediate decision
- No mention of visa sponsorship

BRIDGE arranges and supports all interviews — we will help you prepare and can be present during the call if needed.

posts.title: Resume & CV Guide — How to Stand Out to Korean Schools
posts.body:
# Resume & CV Guide for Teaching in Korea

Your resume is the first thing a school director sees. In Korea, **presentation matters as much as content**. Here's how to make yours stand out.

## Format
- **1 page max** — Korean directors are busy and prefer concise resumes
- Use a clean, professional template (no fancy colors or graphics)
- **Include a professional photo** in the top-right corner (passport-style, smiling)
- PDF format only — Word docs can break formatting on Korean computers

## Must-Have Sections
1. **Personal Information**: Full name, nationality, date of birth, current location
2. **Education**: University name, degree, major, graduation date
3. **Teaching Experience**: Most recent first, include school name + city + dates + age group taught
4. **Certifications**: TEFL/TESOL/CELTA with hours and institution
5. **Skills**: Classroom management, curriculum development, technology skills

## What Korean Schools Actually Look For
- **Photo quality** — Dress professionally, smile naturally
- **Stability** — Completed contracts (no mid-contract runners)
- **Age group experience** — Match what the school teaches
- **Korean experience** — Even 6 months counts significantly

## Common Mistakes
- Listing irrelevant work experience (barista, retail) — only include if you have no teaching experience
- Generic objective statements — be specific about Korea
- Typos or grammar errors — have someone proofread
- No photo — this is considered incomplete in Korea

## Pro Tip
Tailor your resume for each application. If applying to a kindergarten, highlight experience with young learners. For an academy, emphasize after-school experience.

posts.title: What to Pack for Korea — The Essential Packing List
posts.body:
# What to Pack for Teaching in Korea

After 10+ years of placing teachers, here's what you actually need (and what you can skip).

## Bring From Home
- **Documents**: Originals of degree, apostilled CBC, TEFL cert (keep copies separate)
- **Deodorant**: Hard to find Western brands in Korea — bring a year's supply
- **Medications**: Prescription meds + doctor's note in English; allergy meds, pain relievers
- **Shoes above size 280mm (US 11+)**: Large sizes are rare and expensive
- **Clothes above XL**: Finding larger Western sizes is difficult
- **Comfort items**: Favorite snacks, family photos, a good book for the plane

## Skip These — Buy in Korea
- **Electronics**: Korea has cheaper phones, adapters, cables
- **Bedding**: Your apartment will have basics; buy quality locally
- **Winter coat**: Korean paddings (long puffer coats) are affordable and made for Korean winters
- **Toiletries**: Korean skincare and hygiene products are excellent and cheap
- **Kitchen items**: Your apartment should be furnished; Daiso has everything else for 1,000 won

## Clothing Tips
- **Work clothes**: Business casual — collared shirts, slacks, modest dresses
- **No visible tattoos**: Some schools are strict — bring clothes that cover them
- **Indoor shoes**: Many schools require separate indoor footwear
- **Layers**: Korean buildings are well-heated but outdoor temps vary dramatically

## Tech Essentials
- **Unlocked phone**: Get a Korean SIM at the airport
- **Laptop**: For lesson planning and video calls home
- **USB drive**: Korean schools still use them frequently
- **Power adapter**: Korea uses Type C/F (European-style round, 220V)

## Pro Tips
- Pack light — you'll want to shop in Korea (fashion is amazing and affordable)
- Ship a box via sea mail (takes 4-6 weeks but cheap for heavy items)
- Download Papago and Naver Map before you arrive

posts.title: First Week Survival Guide — Your First 7 Days in Korea
posts.body:
# Your First Week in Korea — Survival Guide

The first week can be overwhelming. Here's a day-by-day guide to get settled fast.

## Day 1-2: Immediate Essentials
1. **Get a Korean phone number** — Visit any SKT, KT, or LG U+ store with your passport
2. **Get cash** — ATMs at convenience stores (GS25, CU, 7-Eleven) work with foreign cards
3. **Download essential apps**:
   - **Naver Map** (NOT Google Maps — it doesn't work properly in Korea)
   - **Papago** (translation)
   - **KakaoTalk** (everyone uses it, including your school)
   - **Coupang** (Korean Amazon)
   - **배달의민족 (Baedal Minjok)** or **Coupang Eats** (food delivery)

## Day 3-4: Admin Tasks
4. **Open a bank account** — Go with a Korean-speaking colleague; bring passport + ARC (or come back after ARC)
5. **Register at immigration** — Your school should help with ARC application
6. **Health check** — Required for your ARC; your school will arrange this
7. **Learn your neighborhood** — Find the nearest convenience store, subway station, and laundromat

## Day 5-7: Get Comfortable
8. **Stock your apartment** — Visit Daiso (cheap household goods) and a local mart
9. **Try the school cafeteria** — Most schools provide lunch
10. **Explore your neighborhood** — Walk around, find coffee shops, restaurants
11. **Set up internet banking** — Your bank can help (bring your phone)

## Cultural Quick Tips
- **Bow slightly** when greeting older people or your boss
- **Remove shoes** when entering homes and some restaurants
- **Don't tip** — It's not expected and can even be rude
- **Use two hands** when giving/receiving from elders
- **Trash separation** is serious — learn the system from day one

## Emergency Numbers
- Police: 112
- Fire/Ambulance: 119
- Immigration Helpline: 1345 (multilingual)
- BRIDGE Support: Check your welcome email for our direct line

posts.title: Classroom Management — Tips from Experienced Teachers
posts.body:
# Classroom Management in Korean Schools

Managing a classroom in Korea is different from Western teaching. Here's what actually works.

## For Kindergarten (3-7 years)
- **Routine is everything** — Same greeting song, same warmup, same structure daily
- **Use visual timers** — "When this timer runs out, we move to the next activity"
- **Reward systems work** — Sticker charts, stamp cards, small prizes
- **Energy management** — Alternate between active games and calm activities
- **Korean co-teacher** — Work closely with them; they handle discipline and parent communication

## For Elementary (8-13 years)
- **Be fun but firm** — These kids test boundaries constantly
- **Games with purpose** — Every game should reinforce the lesson target
- **Team competitions** — Korean kids are naturally competitive; use it
- **Phone/tablet policy** — Establish rules from day one
- **Positive reinforcement** — Public praise goes further than public correction

## For Middle/High School
- **Respect their time** — Teens are exhausted from their schedules
- **Make it relevant** — Pop culture, music, trending topics
- **Less textbook, more conversation** — They get enough textbook at school
- **Don't take attitude personally** — It's usually exhaustion, not disrespect
- **Group work** — Shy students participate more in small groups

## Universal Tips
- **Learn student names fast** — Use name tags for the first month
- **Volume control** — Use countdown methods, clap patterns, or whisper technique
- **Never yell** — It damages your relationship and doesn't work long-term
- **Prepare 20% more material** — Better to have too much than too little
- **Start strict, ease up later** — Much easier than the reverse

## Cultural Notes
- Korean students may be quiet — it's cultural, not disinterest
- Physical punishment is **illegal** — never touch a student
- Some students will call you "Teacher" not your name — this is respectful
- End-of-class bowing is normal — bow back

posts.title: Korean Language Basics — 20 Phrases Every Teacher Needs
posts.body:
# Korean for ESL Teachers — 20 Essential Phrases

You don't need to be fluent, but these phrases will make your daily life much easier.

## At School
1. **안녕하세요** (annyeonghaseyo) — Hello (formal)
2. **감사합니다** (gamsahamnida) — Thank you (formal)
3. **죄송합니다** (joesonghamnida) — I'm sorry (formal)
4. **화장실 어디예요?** (hwajangsil eodiyeyo?) — Where is the bathroom?
5. **네 / 아니요** (ne / aniyo) — Yes / No
6. **잠깐만요** (jamkkanmanyo) — Just a moment
7. **이해 못 해요** (ihae mot haeyo) — I don't understand

## Daily Life
8. **이거 얼마예요?** (igeo eolmayeyo?) — How much is this?
9. **이거 주세요** (igeo juseyo) — This one please
10. **영수증 주세요** (yeongsujeung juseyo) — Receipt please
11. **카드 돼요?** (kadeu dwaeyo?) — Can I pay by card?
12. **매워요?** (maewoyo?) — Is it spicy?
13. **안 맵게 해주세요** (an maepge haejuseyo) — Not spicy please
14. **포장이요** (pojangniyo) — To go / takeout

## Transportation
15. **여기 세워주세요** (yeogi sewojuseyo) — Stop here please (taxi)
16. **얼마나 걸려요?** (eolmana geollyeoyo?) — How long does it take?

## Social
17. **괜찮아요** (gwaenchanayo) — It's okay / I'm fine
18. **맛있어요** (masisseoyo) — It's delicious
19. **건배!** (geonbae!) — Cheers!
20. **다음에 또 봐요** (daeume tto bwayo) — See you next time

## Learning Tips
- **Hangul takes 2-3 hours to learn** — The Korean alphabet is logical and phonetic
- Use the **Talk To Me In Korean** (TTMIK) app — it's free and excellent
- Practice with your Korean co-teacher — they'll appreciate the effort
- KakaoTalk has a built-in translator for messages
- Many Korean words come from English: 커피 (keopi = coffee), 버스 (beoseu = bus)

## Why It Matters
Even basic Korean effort earns massive respect from your colleagues and students. Schools love teachers who try to integrate, and your daily life becomes 10x easier.

posts.title: Saving Money in Korea — Budget Tips for Teachers
posts.body:
# How to Save Money Teaching in Korea

One of the biggest perks of teaching in Korea is the saving potential. Here's how to maximize it.

## Your Monthly Budget (Typical)
| Category | Budget (KRW) | Notes |
|----------|-------------|-------|
| Rent | 0 (school-provided) | Or 400-600K if housing allowance |
| Food | 300-500K | Cooking at home saves a lot |
| Transport | 50-100K | T-money card, subway + bus |
| Phone | 30-50K | Prepaid plans are cheapest |
| Utilities | 50-150K | Electric, gas, water, internet |
| Social/Fun | 200-400K | Dining out, drinks, activities |
| **Total** | **630K-1.2M** | |

## With a 2.5M salary, you can save 1.0-1.5M per month

## Top Saving Tips
1. **Cook at home** — Korean grocery stores are affordable; rice, eggs, and vegetables are cheap
2. **School lunch** — Many schools provide free lunch; that's 22 meals/month saved
3. **Public transport** — Subway + bus is cheap and efficient; skip taxis
4. **Coupang Rocket Delivery** — Free next-day delivery; compare prices before buying in stores
5. **Avoid Itaewon/Gangnam prices** — Local Korean restaurants are 5,000-8,000 won per meal vs 15,000+ in expat areas
6. **Korean haircuts** — 10,000-15,000 won vs 40,000+ at "foreigner" salons

## Banking & Transfers
- **Wise (TransferWise)** — Best exchange rates for sending money home
- **Toss** — Korean fintech app; great for splitting bills and cashback
- **National Pension refund** — You get your pension contributions back when you leave Korea (most nationalities)

## Hidden Income
- **Severance pay** — After 1 year, you get 1 month's salary as severance
- **Flight reimbursement** — Many contracts include round-trip airfare
- **Housing deposit return** — Any deposit you put down comes back
- **Tax refund** — File before you leave; many teachers overpay taxes

## What to Avoid
- **Expensive coffee shops daily** — Make coffee at home (4,500 won/day = 135,000 won/month)
- **Convenience store meals** — Cooking is cheaper and healthier
- **ATM fees** — Use your Korean bank's ATMs, not foreign ones
- **Impulse shopping** — Korean online shopping is addictive; set a monthly limit

posts.title: Weekend Trips — Best Destinations Near Your City
posts.body:
# Weekend Trip Guide for Teachers in Korea

Korea is small enough that you can visit almost anywhere in a weekend. Here are the best trips by season.

## Spring (March - May)
- **Jinhae Cherry Blossom Festival** — The most famous cherry blossom spot in Korea (Busan area)
- **Gyeongju** — Ancient capital with temples, tombs, and cherry blossoms
- **Boseong Green Tea Fields** — Stunning terraced tea plantations

## Summer (June - August)
- **Busan Beaches** — Haeundae and Gwangalli are iconic
- **Jeju Island** — Korea's Hawaii; fly for as low as 30,000 won on budget airlines
- **Sokcho & Seoraksan** — Mountain hiking and fresh seafood on the east coast
- **Boryeong Mud Festival** — The biggest foreigner-friendly festival

## Fall (September - November)
- **Seoraksan National Park** — Best autumn foliage in Korea
- **Naejangsan** — Stunning maple tunnel
- **Andong Hahoe Village** — Traditional Korean village + mask dance festival
- **DMZ Tour** — Book in advance; fascinating history

## Winter (December - February)
- **Ski Resorts** — Pyeongchang, Yongpyong, High1 Resort (lift + rental from 60,000 won)
- **Busan Christmas Market** — Less crowded than Seoul in winter
- **Jeonju Hanok Village** — Traditional village with amazing street food
- **Ice Fishing Festivals** — Hwacheon or Pyeongchang

## Budget Travel Tips
- **KTX** — Korea's bullet train; Seoul to Busan in 2.5 hours
- **Express Bus** — Cheaper than KTX; comfortable and frequent
- **Airbnb/Guesthouse** — 30-50,000 won/night; way cheaper than hotels
- **T-money card** — Works on all buses and subways nationwide

## Day Trips from Seoul

posts.title: Video Interview Tips — Ace Your School Interview
posts.body:
# How to Ace Your Korean School Interview

Most interviews are conducted via Google Meet or Zoom. Here's what to expect and how to prepare.

## What They Ask (90% of interviews)
1. **Tell me about yourself** — Keep it 2 minutes; teaching background + why Korea
2. **Why do you want to teach in Korea?** — Be genuine; mention culture, career growth
3. **What age group do you prefer?** — Match the school's needs if possible
4. **Describe your teaching style** — Use specific examples
5. **How would you handle a difficult student?** — Show patience and strategy
6. **What would you do if you couldn't speak Korean and needed help?** — Show resourcefulness
7. **Do you have any questions for us?** — Always ask at least 2-3 questions

## Technical Setup
- **Camera**: Eye level, well-lit room (face a window or use a desk lamp)
- **Background**: Clean and neutral (no messy beds or posters)
- **Audio**: Use headphones with a mic; test audio before the call
- **Internet**: Wired connection if possible; close other apps/tabs
- **Dress**: Business casual from head to toe (you might need to stand up)

## What Schools Look For
- **Enthusiasm** — Smile, be energetic, show genuine interest
- **Reliability** — Will this person complete their contract?
- **Flexibility** — Can they adapt to Korean school culture?
- **Professionalism** — Are they punctual, prepared, and polite?
- **Clear speech** — Can students understand them easily?

## Questions to Ask the School
- What curriculum or textbooks do you use?
- How many students per class?
- What does a typical day look like?
- Is there a Korean co-teacher in the classroom?
- What support do you offer for new teachers?
- What is the housing like?

## Red Flags to Watch For
- They won't discuss salary or benefits clearly
- No contract before you fly
- "We'll figure out housing when you arrive"
- Extremely vague job description
- Pressure to decide immediately

## After the Interview
- Send a brief thank-you email within 24 hours
- If you don't hear back in 3-5 days, follow up once
- BRIDGE will coordinate between you and the school

posts.title: Contract Red Flags — What to Check Before Signing
posts.body:
# Understanding Your Korean Teaching Contract

Before signing anything, make sure you understand every clause. Here's what to look for.

## Must-Have Clauses
- **Salary**: Exact monthly amount in KRW (before tax)
- **Working hours**: Daily start/end time, total weekly teaching hours
- **Housing**: Free single housing OR housing allowance amount
- **Vacation**: Minimum 10 paid days + national holidays
- **Severance**: 1 month salary after 12 months (required by Korean law)
- **Flight**: At least one-way; most offer round-trip
- **Health insurance**: 50/50 split employer-employee (required by law)
- **National pension**: 50/50 split (required by law)
- **Contract period**: Start date and end date (usually 12 months)

## Red Flags
- **"Salary negotiable"** — Get the exact number in writing
- **"Up to 30 teaching hours"** — This usually means exactly 30; negotiate if too high
- **No mention of overtime pay** — Any work beyond contract hours must be compensated
- **"Housing deposit from your salary"** — School should cover the deposit
- **"6-month probation"** — Unusual for ESL; negotiate to remove or reduce
- **Penalty clauses** — Check what happens if you leave early; excessive penalties are a red flag
- **"You will be responsible for..."** — Vague duties can mean anything

## Korean Labor Law Protections
- Maximum 40 hours/week (52 with overtime)
- Overtime must be compensated at 150% rate
- Severance pay is **legally required** after 1 year
- You cannot be fired without cause
- 4 major insurances are mandatory (health, pension, employment, accident)

## What BRIDGE Recommends
1. **Read every line** before signing
2. Get the contract in **both English and Korean** — the Korean version is legally binding
3. Ask about the **Letter of Release** policy in case of disputes
4. **Never pay a recruiter fee** — legitimate recruiters are paid by the school
5. Send your contract to BRIDGE for a **free review** before signing

posts.title: Cultural Do's and Don'ts — Korean Workplace Culture
posts.body:
# Korean Workplace Culture for Foreign Teachers

Understanding Korean workplace norms will make your life much smoother.

## Do's
- **Arrive early** — Being "on time" means 5-10 minutes early in Korea
- **Greet everyone** — Say hello to every colleague when you arrive
- **Join dinners** — Staff dinners (회식 hoesik) are important for bonding
- **Accept gifts graciously** — Korean colleagues often share food and small gifts
- **Dress conservatively** — Even on casual days, avoid shorts, tank tops, or revealing clothing
- **Show interest in Korean culture** — Ask about holidays, try Korean food, learn basic phrases

## Don'ts
- **Don't leave before your boss** — Wait until senior staff leave (or ask permission)
- **Don't refuse soju** — At least accept the first glass; it's okay to sip slowly
- **Don't call in sick casually** — Koreans have very different sick day culture
- **Don't argue publicly** — Save disagreements for private conversations
- **Don't eat at your desk during class hours** — Use break time
- **Don't be too casual with the director** — Maintain respect hierarchy

## Understanding 눈치 (Nunchi)
Nunchi is the Korean concept of "reading the room." It means:
- Noticing when others are busy and not interrupting
- Picking up on subtle hints rather than waiting for direct instructions
- Adapting your behavior to the situation
- Understanding that "maybe" often means "no"

## Hierarchy in Korean Schools
- **Director (원장님)**: Ultimate authority — always show respect
- **Head Teacher**: Your direct supervisor — go to them with issues
- **Korean Co-teacher**: Your daily partner — build a strong relationship
- **Other Staff**: Janitors, bus drivers, kitchen staff — be friendly to everyone

## Holiday Awareness
- **Seollal (Lunar New Year)** — 3-day holiday, usually February
- **Chuseok** — Korean Thanksgiving, 3-day holiday, usually September/October
- **Children's Day (May 5)** — Schools are closed
- **Teacher's Day (May 15)** — You'll receive gifts and appreciation

## When Things Go Wrong
- Talk to your Korean co-teacher first
- If unresolved, speak with your recruiter (BRIDGE)
- Document everything in writing (email/KakaoTalk)
- Korean labor board (고용노동부) handles serious disputes: call 1350

posts.title: Health & Fitness — Staying Healthy in Korea
posts.body:
# Staying Healthy as a Teacher in Korea

## Healthcare System
Korea has excellent, affordable healthcare. With your national health insurance:
- **Doctor visit**: 5,000-15,000 won copay
- **Specialist**: 15,000-30,000 won
- **Hospital stay**: Very affordable compared to Western countries
- **Dental**: Covered for basics; cosmetic is out-of-pocket but still cheap
- **Pharmacy**: Right next to most clinics; show your prescription

## Finding a Doctor
- **Use Naver Map** to search "병원" (hospital) or specific specialties
- Many clinics near universities have English-speaking doctors
- **International clinics** in Seoul: Severance Hospital, Samsung Medical Center
- Walk-ins are common — no appointment needed for most clinics

## Mental Health
Teaching abroad can be isolating. Take care of your mental health:
- **Seoul Global Center**: Free counseling in English (02-2075-4180)
- **Crisis helpline**: 1393 (Korean), 1588-9191 (multilingual)
- Many schools offer EAP (Employee Assistance Programs)
- Join expat groups on Facebook or Reddit for community support

## Staying Fit
- **Gym memberships**: 50-80,000 won/month (much cheaper than Western countries)
- **Korean hiking culture**: Mountains are everywhere; well-maintained trails
- **Han River parks**: Free running/cycling paths (bike rental 1,000 won/hour)
- **Public pools**: 5,000-8,000 won per visit
- **Yoga/Pilates studios**: Popular and affordable

## Food & Nutrition
- Korean food is generally healthy — lots of vegetables, fermented foods, lean protein
- **Watch the sodium** — Korean food can be very salty
- **Meal prep** — Buy in bulk at Costco or Homeplus
- **Western food cravings**: Coupang sells imported foods; Itaewon/Haebangchon have Western restaurants

## Common Health Issues for Teachers
- **Voice strain** — Tea with honey, throat lozenges, learn to project without straining
- **Back pain** — Many teachers sit on low Korean-style furniture
- **Weight gain** — School lunches are large; portions in Korea can be big
- **Seasonal allergies** — Yellow dust (황사) in spring; wear a mask on bad days
- **Air quality** — Check the AirVisual app daily; wear KF94 masks when needed

posts.title: TEFL vs TESOL vs CELTA — Which Certificate Do You Need?
posts.body:
# Teaching Certifications Explained

Confused about which certification to get? Here's a straightforward comparison.

## Quick Answer
For most Korean academy/school positions: **Any 120+ hour TEFL/TESOL is fine.**

## Comparison

### TEFL (Teaching English as a Foreign Language)
- **Hours**: 120-150 hours typical
- **Cost**: $200-500 online
- **Time**: 1-3 months self-paced
- **Best for**: Academy (hagwon) jobs, most Korean positions
- **Where**: International TEFL Academy, i-to-i, Bridge (unrelated to us!)

### TESOL (Teaching English to Speakers of Other Languages)
- **Hours**: 120-150 hours typical
- **Cost**: $200-600
- **Time**: Similar to TEFL
- **Best for**: Same as TEFL — the names are essentially interchangeable
- **Note**: Some university programs offer TESOL master's degrees

### CELTA (Certificate in English Language Teaching to Adults)
- **Hours**: 120 hours intensive
- **Cost**: $1,500-2,500
- **Time**: 4-5 weeks full-time or part-time over several months
- **Best for**: International schools, university positions, career teachers
- **Where**: British Council centers, authorized training centers
- **Note**: Includes observed teaching practice — much more rigorous

### Teaching License
- Full government teaching credential from your home country
- **Best for**: International schools, EPIK, public schools
- **Highest salary bracket** in most positions

## What Korean Schools Actually Require
- **Academies (Hagwon)**: TEFL/TESOL preferred but not always required
- **Public Schools (EPIK/GEPIK)**: TEFL preferred; gives application points
- **International Schools**: CELTA/teaching license often required
- **Universities**: Master's degree + CELTA or equivalent usually required

## Our Recommendation
1. If you're new to teaching: Get a **120-hour online TEFL** before applying
2. If you want a career in ESL: Invest in **CELTA**
3. If you already have teaching experience: Your resume matters more than certificates
4. **Avoid** certificates under 100 hours — many schools won't accept them

posts.title: Document Apostille Guide for Teaching in Korea
posts.body:
## How to Apostille Your Documents for a Korean Teaching Visa

An **apostille** is an international certification that authenticates documents for use in countries that are members of the Hague Convention. South Korea requires apostilled documents for E-2 visa applications.

### Which Documents Need Apostille?

- **Bachelor's degree diploma** (original or notarized copy, depending on your country)
- **Criminal background check** (national-level)
- **University transcripts** (if required by your consulate)

### Country-Specific Apostille Authorities

| Country | Apostille Authority | Website |
|---------|-------------------|----------|
| **USA** | U.S. Department of State | travel.state.gov |
| **Canada** | Global Affairs Canada* | international.gc.ca |
| **UK** | Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office | gov.uk/get-document-legalised |
| **Australia** | DFAT | smartraveller.gov.au |
| **Ireland** | Department of Foreign Affairs | dfa.ie |
| **New Zealand** | Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade | mfat.govt.nz |
| **South Africa** | DIRCO | dirco.gov.za |

*Canada uses **authentication and legalization** rather than apostille, but the process is equivalent.

### Step-by-Step Process (US Example)

1. **Obtain your documents** — Request your FBI background check and get a certified copy of your degree if needed.
2. **Check notarization requirements** — Some states require documents to be notarized by a public notary before apostille.
3. **Submit to the U.S. Department of State** — Mail your documents with the completed application form (DS-4194) and the processing fee.
4. **Wait for processing** — Standard processing takes approximately **4-6 weeks**. Expedited service may be available.
5. **Receive apostilled documents** — Documents will be returned with the apostille certificate attached.

### Using a Third-Party Service

Many teachers use apostille expediting services to save time. These services can often complete the process in **1-2 weeks** but charge additional fees ($50-$150+ per document). Reputable options include:

- **One Source Process** (US-based)
- **Washington Express Visas**

### Important Tips

- **Never separate** the apostille from the document it is attached to.
- **Make copies** of everything before mailing originals.
- Some Korean consulates accept **digital apostilles** (e-Apostilles); verify with your specific consulate.
- If your degree is from a country different from your citizenship, the apostille must come from the **country that issued the document**.
- Plan for the apostille process to take **4-8 weeks total** when budgeting your timeline.

posts.title: FBI Background Check Process for ESL Teachers
posts.body:
## FBI Criminal Background Check: A Guide for US Citizens Teaching in Korea

US citizens applying for an E-2 teaching visa in Korea must submit an **FBI Identity History Summary** (commonly called an FBI background check). Here is a complete walkthrough.

### Option 1: Online via FBI Website (Recommended)

The fastest method is the **electronic application**:

1. Visit [https://www.edo.cjis.gov](https://www.edo.cjis.gov)
2. Create an account and complete the online form
3. Schedule an appointment at an **approved channeler** or local police station for electronic fingerprinting
4. Pay the **$18 processing fee** online
5. Results are typically returned in **3-5 business days**

### Option 2: Mail-In with FD-258 Card

If electronic submission is not available in your area:

1. Obtain an **FD-258 fingerprint card** (available from most police departments)
2. Get your fingerprints taken at a local police station or UPS Store
3. Mail the completed card to the FBI with a **$18 money order** or certified check
4. Processing takes approximately **12-16 weeks**

### After Receiving Your Results

Once you have your FBI background check:

1. **Review it carefully** — Ensure all information is correct and there are no errors
2. **Get it apostilled** — Submit it to the U.S. Department of State for an apostille (see our Apostille Guide)
3. **Check the expiration** — Most Korean consulates require the background check to be **less than 6 months old** at the time of visa application

### Frequently Asked Questions

**Q: What if I have a minor offense on my record?**
A: Minor traffic violations typically do not disqualify you. However, any drug-related offenses, DUIs, or violent crimes will likely result in visa denial. Consult with your prospective employer and the Korean consulate.

**Q: I live abroad. Can I still get an FBI check?**
Yes. You can use an **approved FBI channeler** that accepts international submissions, or have a trusted person in the US mail the application on your behalf. Many channelers offer ink-card scanning services remotely.

**Q: My background check has my old name. Is that a problem?**
Yes. If your name has changed, you will need to include a **notarized name change document** (marriage certificate, court order, etc.) with your visa application.

### Pro Tips

- **Start this process first** — it is almost always the bottleneck in your visa timeline.
- Use an **approved channeler** like Fieldprint or Identogo for the fastest electronic processing.
- Keep the **original** for apostille and make digital scans for your records.
- If results show "No Record," that is the ideal outcome.

posts.title: E-2 Visa Extension Procedures in South Korea
posts.body:
## How to Extend Your E-2 Teaching Visa in Korea

If you are continuing to teach in Korea beyond your initial contract, you will need to extend your E-2 visa. The process is handled **in-country** through your local Immigration Office.

### When to Apply

- Apply **no earlier than 4 months** and **no later than 1 month** before your current visa expires.
- If you apply after your visa expires, you may face fines and potential deportation.

### Required Documents

1. **Application Form** (Form 34, available at the immigration office or on HiKorea)
2. **Passport** (original)
3. **Alien Registration Card (ARC)**
4. **New or renewed employment contract** — signed by both you and your employer
5. **Business Registration Certificate** of your employer (copy)
6. **Fee**: 60,000 KRW (subject to change)

### Documents Your Employer Must Provide

- Copy of the **business registration certificate**
- **Employment confirmation letter** on company letterhead
- **Tax payment certificate** of the institution

### Step-by-Step Process

1. **Gather documents** — Collect all required documents from both yourself and your employer.
2. **Book an appointment** — Reserve a time slot on [HiKorea](https://www.hikorea.go.kr) (highly recommended to avoid long waits).
3. **Visit your local Immigration Office** — Go to the office that corresponds to your registered address.
4. **Submit your application** — Hand in all documents and pay the fee.
5. **Wait for processing** — Extensions are typically processed within **1-3 weeks**. Your passport may be held during this time, so request a **receipt** that serves as temporary proof of legal stay.

### Changing Employers During Extension

If you are switching to a new school while extending:

- You need a **Letter of Release** (LOR) from your current employer.
- The new employer must submit a **new contract** and their business registration.
- If your current employer refuses to issue an LOR, contact the **Labor Board** (1350 helpline) for assistance.

### Important Notes

- **You do not need a new background check** for extensions, as long as you are renewing with the same type of visa.
- Your extension period will typically match your new **contract length** (usually 1 year).
- If you have changed your address since your last registration, update it **before** applying for extension.
- Keep your ARC updated with your current address at all times — failure to report address changes within 14 days can result in fines.

### Online Application Option

Some extension applications can now be submitted via **HiKorea online**. Check the platform for eligibility based on your specific situation.

posts.title: Common Visa Rejection Reasons and How to Avoid Them
posts.body:
## Common E-2 Visa Rejection Reasons and How to Avoid Them

Having your visa application rejected is frustrating and can delay your plans by weeks or months. Here are the most common reasons for E-2 visa rejections and practical steps to avoid them.

### 1. Criminal Record Issues

**Problem**: Any drug-related offense, DUI, or violent crime on your record.

**How to avoid**: 
- Obtain your background check **early** so you know what it says before applying.
- If you have a minor offense, consult with the Korean consulate **before** submitting your application.
- Some offenses from many years ago may be eligible for **expungement** in your home country.

### 2. Expired or Invalid Documents

**Problem**: Documents older than the accepted validity period (usually 6 months for background checks).

**How to avoid**:
- Check the **specific validity requirements** of your target consulate.
- Time your document preparation so everything is **fresh** when you apply.
- Background checks and apostilles should ideally be **less than 3 months old** at submission.

### 3. Degree Issues

**Problem**: Degree not from an accredited institution, degree is not a 4-year bachelor's, or degree is from an online-only institution that is not recognized.

**How to avoid**:
- Verify your university's **accreditation status** through your country's education department.
- If you attended multiple institutions, ensure your **final degree-granting institution** is accredited.
- Associate degrees and 3-year degrees **do not qualify** for E-2 visas.

### 4. Incorrect Apostille

**Problem**: Document apostilled by the wrong authority or apostille from a non-Hague Convention country.

**How to avoid**:
- Ensure the apostille is issued by the **correct national authority** (see our Apostille Guide).
- The apostille must be for the **same document** it is attached to.
- Do not separate, fold, or damage the apostille.

### 5. Employer/Contract Problems

**Problem**: The hiring institution has a poor record with Immigration, the contract does not meet minimum requirements, or the employer's business registration is invalid.

**How to avoid**:
- Research your employer thoroughly before signing.
- Ensure your contract includes **minimum salary requirements** (varies by region).
- Ask the employer if they have **successfully sponsored E-2 visas** before.

### 6. Incomplete Application

**Problem**: Missing documents, incorrect form, or missing photographs.

**How to avoid**:
- Use a **checklist** and double-check every item before submission.
- Call the consulate to confirm the **exact requirements** — they can vary by location.
- Bring **extra copies** of everything.

### 7. Nationality Issues

**Problem**: Applicant is not a citizen of one of the seven designated English-speaking countries.

**How to avoid**:
- If you hold **dual citizenship** with an eligible country, apply using that passport.
- Non-eligible nationals may qualify for **E-7 (Special Activity)** visas instead, though requirements are different.

### 8. Health Check Failures

**Problem**: Positive drug test, active tuberculosis, or positive HIV test.

**How to avoid**:
- **Absolutely do not use any drugs** before or during the application process. Korea has zero tolerance.
- If you have a health condition, consult with a doctor about how it might affect your application.

### General Best Practices

- **Start early**: Begin the process **3-6 months** before your intended departure.
- **Communicate with your employer**: A good employer will guide you through the process.
- **Keep digital copies**: Scan every document and store securely in cloud storage.
- **Contact the consulate directly**: When in doubt, call or email your target Korean consulate. Requirements can vary between locations and change without notice.
- **Join ESL teacher communities**: Facebook groups and forums like Reddit's r/teachinginkorea are invaluable for up-to-date information from people who have recently gone through the process.

posts.title: Degree & Criminal Record — 학위·범죄경력 공증 총정리
posts.body:
## Degree & Criminal Record Authentication Guide
**학위·범죄경력 공증 총정리**

All foreign documents submitted for an E-2 visa must be **authenticated**. This guide explains the two methods: Apostille and Consular Legalization.

### Apostille vs. Consular Legalization

**Apostille** (for Hague Convention member countries):
- A simplified one-step certification recognized internationally
- All seven E-2 eligible countries (USA, Canada, UK, Ireland, Australia, New Zealand, South Africa) are Hague Convention members
- Issued by a designated government authority (e.g., U.S. Secretary of State, UK FCDO)

**Consular Legalization** (alternative path):
- Notarize the document → Authenticate at the relevant government office → Legalize at the Korean embassy/consulate
- Generally takes longer and costs more than an Apostille

### Degree Authentication Steps

1. **Obtain your original degree** or a certified copy from your university
2. **Get the degree notarized** by a licensed notary public (if required by your country's apostille authority)
3. **Apply for an Apostille** from the designated authority in your country
4. If your degree was issued in a different country than your passport country, additional steps may apply — contact BRIDGE for guidance

### Criminal Background Check Authentication

1. **Request your national-level background check** (see country-specific posts for details)
2. **Apostille the background check** once received
3. Background checks typically have a **6-month validity window**, so plan your timeline carefully

### Common Mistakes to Avoid

- **Do not** get the Apostille before having the document notarized (where notarization is required)
- **Do not** open sealed background check envelopes — some must remain sealed until presented to immigration
- **Do not** wait too long — expired documents must be re-obtained
- **Do not** submit photocopies — originals or certified copies are required

### Cost Estimates

| Item | Approximate Cost |
|------|------------------|
| Apostille (varies by country) | $10 - $100 USD |
| Notarization | $10 - $50 USD |
| Background check | $18 - $75 USD |
| Shipping (expedited) | $30 - $80 USD |

posts.title: After Receiving VIN — 사증발급인정번호 수령 후 절차
posts.body:
## What to Do After Receiving Your VIN
**사증발급인정번호 수령 후 절차**

Congratulations! If your employer has received a **Visa Issuance Number (VIN / 사증발급인정번호)**, you are one step closer to teaching in Korea. Here is exactly what to do next.

### Step 1: Receive Your VIN

- Your employer (or BRIDGE on their behalf) will send you the **VIN confirmation letter** (사증발급인정서)
- This document contains your unique VIN number, which looks like: `2026-0000-0000000`
- The VIN is valid for **3 months** from the date of issuance — you must apply for your visa within this period

### Step 2: Apply for E-2 Visa at Korean Embassy/Consulate

Visit the Korean embassy or consulate in your home country with:

1. **VIN confirmation letter** (original or copy)
2. **Passport** (valid 6+ months)
3. **Visa application form** (download from embassy website)
4. **Passport photo** (3.5cm x 4.5cm)
5. **Application fee** (varies by country, typically $40-80 USD)

*Some embassies allow mail-in applications. Check your local embassy website for details.*

### Step 3: Receive Your Visa

Processing usually takes **3-5 business days**. Your visa will be a sticker placed in your passport.

### Step 4: Book Your Flight

- Coordinate your arrival date with your employer
- Your E-2 visa allows a **single entry** initially
- Make sure to arrive **before your contract start date** to allow time for settling in

### Step 5: After Arrival in Korea

1. **Medical Examination** — Complete within 2 weeks of arrival at a designated hospital
2. **Alien Registration Card (ARC)** — Apply within 90 days at your local immigration office
3. **Bank Account** — Open a Korean bank account (requires ARC)
4. **Phone Number** — Get a Korean SIM card or phone plan

### Timeline Summary

| Step | Duration |
|------|----------|
| VIN received → Visa application | 1-2 weeks |
| Visa processing | 3-5 business days |
| Travel to Korea | Coordinate with employer |
| Medical exam + ARC | Within 90 days of arrival |

posts.title: USA — FBI Background Check 범죄경력조회
posts.body:
## USA — FBI Background Check for E-2 Visa
**범죄경력조회 (미국)**

U.S. citizens applying for an E-2 visa must obtain a **federal-level** background check from the FBI. State-level checks are **not accepted**.

### Option 1: FBI Channeler (Recommended — Fastest)

FBI-approved channelers can process your background check in as little as **1-3 business days**.

1. Visit an FBI-approved channeler website (e.g., Fieldprint, Identogo)
2. Schedule a fingerprinting appointment or request a fingerprint card
3. Submit fingerprints electronically
4. Receive results via email or mail within **1-5 business days**
5. Cost: approximately **$50-60 USD**

### Option 2: Directly Through the FBI

1. Obtain a **fingerprint card** (FD-258) from a local police station or UPS store
2. Mail the completed card to the FBI CJIS Division in Clarksburg, WV
3. Include a money order or certified check for **$18 USD**
4. Processing time: **12-16 weeks** (not recommended if you are in a hurry)

### Apostille Process

After receiving your FBI background check:

1. Send the **original** FBI background check to the **U.S. Department of State** for Apostille
2. You can apply online via the Department of State website or mail it in
3. Processing time: **4-8 weeks** by mail, faster if using an expediting service
4. Cost: **$20 USD** (government fee)
5. Expediting services can process in **1-5 business days** for an additional fee ($100-200)

### Tips

- **Start early** — the FBI check + Apostille process can take 2-3 months if done by mail
- **Use an expediting service** if your timeline is tight (companies like G3 Visas, International Documents Services)
- Keep your background check **sealed** if it arrives in a sealed envelope
- The background check is valid for **6 months** — plan accordingly
- If you are already in Korea, you can get fingerprinted at the U.S. Embassy in Seoul and mail them to the FBI or a channeler

### Useful Links

- FBI Identity History Summary: **fbi.gov/how-we-can-help-you**
- U.S. Apostille: **travel.state.gov**

posts.title: UK — ACRO Criminal Background Check 범죄경력
posts.body:
## UK — ACRO Criminal Record Check
**범죄경력조회 (영국)**

UK citizens must obtain a criminal record check through **ACRO Criminal Records Office** (formerly the ACPO). A standard DBS check is **not accepted** for overseas use.

### How to Apply

1. Visit **acro.police.uk** and select "Police Certificates"
2. Create an account and complete the online application
3. Provide your personal details, including all addresses for the past 10 years
4. Pay the fee: **£55 GBP** (as of 2025)
5. Processing time: **10-15 business days** (can take longer during busy periods)

### Required Information

- Full name (including any previous names)
- Date of birth
- Address history for the past 10 years
- Valid email address
- Debit/credit card for payment

### Apostille Process (FCDO)

Once you receive your ACRO certificate:

1. Apply for an **Apostille** through the **Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO)**
2. Apply online at **gov.uk/get-document-legalised**
3. Fee: **£30 GBP** for the standard service
4. Standard processing: **2 weeks** from receipt
5. Premium (in-person, London office): **same day** — £75 GBP

### Alternative: Expediting Services

Several private companies can handle both the ACRO application and FCDO Apostille on your behalf. This is useful if you are already abroad and cannot easily visit UK offices.

### Important Notes

- An ACRO certificate is **different** from a DBS check — make sure you apply for the correct one
- The certificate will list any **unspent convictions and cautions**
- If your name has changed (e.g., through marriage), include all previous names
- ACRO certificates do not expire per se, but Korean immigration typically requires documents issued within **6 months**
- Allow plenty of time — ACRO can be slow during peak periods (summer, January)

### Contact

- ACRO: **acro.police.uk** / +44 (0)23 8068 2648
- FCDO Legalisation Office: **gov.uk/get-document-legalised**

posts.title: UK — KVAC London 비자신청센터 안내
posts.body:
## UK — Korean Visa Application Centre (KVAC) London
**비자신청센터 안내 (영국)**

UK-based applicants apply for Korean visas through the **Korean Visa Application Centre (KVAC)** in London, not directly at the Korean Embassy.

### KVAC London Details

- **Address**: 1-2 Berners Street, London, W1T 3LA
- **Operating Hours**: Monday to Friday, 9:00 AM - 4:00 PM (application submission); 1:00 PM - 4:00 PM (passport collection)
- **Website**: Check the official Korean Embassy UK website for the latest KVAC information

### How to Apply

1. **Prepare your documents** (see the E-2 Visa Requirements post for the full list)
2. **Book an appointment** through the KVAC online booking system (walk-ins may not be accepted)
3. **Visit KVAC** in person with all original documents and photocopies
4. **Pay the visa fee** at KVAC (cash or card)
5. **Collect your passport** when notified, or pay for return delivery

### Documents to Bring

- Passport (valid 6+ months)
- VIN confirmation letter from your Korean employer
- Completed visa application form
- Passport photo (3.5cm x 4.5cm)
- Apostilled degree certificate
- Apostilled ACRO criminal record check
- TEFL/TESOL certificate (if applicable)
- Visa fee

### Processing Time

- Standard: **5-7 working days**
- May take longer during busy periods or if additional review is needed

### Tips for UK Applicants

- **Book early**: KVAC appointment slots fill up quickly, especially in summer
- **Postal applications**: Some applicants outside London may be able to apply by post — check the KVAC website
- **Scottish/Welsh/NI applicants**: You may apply by post if you cannot travel to London
- **Visa fee**: Approximately **£40-50 GBP** (check current rates)
- **Tracking**: KVAC provides a tracking number to check your application status online

### Common Issues

- Forgetting the **VIN letter** — this is essential and cannot be substituted
- Incorrect photo size — make sure it meets Korean requirements (3.5x4.5cm, white background)
- **Expired documents** — ensure your ACRO and degree apostille are within 6 months

posts.title: Australia — Notarization & Apostille 학위·범죄 공증
posts.body:
## Australia — Document Authentication for E-2 Visa
**학위·범죄 공증 (호주)**

Australian citizens need to authenticate their degree and criminal background check for the Korean E-2 visa. Australia is a member of the Hague Apostille Convention (effective since 2024).

### Criminal Background Check (AFP)

Australia uses the **Australian Federal Police (AFP)** for national criminal history checks.

1. Visit **afp.gov.au** and apply for a **National Police Check**
2. Select purpose: **Employment — overseas**
3. Complete the online application and provide **100 points of identification**
4. Pay the fee: **AUD $42** (standard) or **AUD $131** (priority — 2 business days)
5. Standard processing: **10-15 business days**

### Degree Authentication

1. Obtain your **original degree** or a certified copy from your university
2. Have it **notarized** by an Australian Notary Public
3. Apply for an **Apostille** from DFAT (see below)

### Apostille Process (DFAT)

Since Australia joined the Hague Apostille Convention in **January 2024**, the process has been simplified:

1. Apply through the **Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade (DFAT)**
2. Submit via the **DFAT Apostille Online Portal** or by mail
3. Fee: **AUD $108** per document
4. Processing time: **5-10 business days** (online), longer by mail
5. Documents must first be **notarized** by an Australian Notary Public before Apostille

### Important Notes

- **Pre-2024 documents**: If your documents were legalized before Australia joined the Convention, they may still be accepted — check with BRIDGE
- **Certified copies**: Some documents can be authenticated as certified copies rather than originals — check with DFAT
- **AFP checks from overseas**: If you are not in Australia, you can still apply online and have results mailed to an overseas address
- Processing times may vary — start **at least 6 weeks** before your planned departure

### Useful Contacts

- AFP National Police Checks: **afp.gov.au**
- DFAT Apostille: **dfat.gov.au/apostille**
- Korean Embassy Canberra: **overseas.mofa.go.kr/au-en**

posts.title: E-2 Visa Requirements Overview for ESL Teachers in Korea
posts.body:
## E-2 Teaching Visa: Complete Requirements Guide

The **E-2 visa** is the standard work visa for foreign English teachers in South Korea. Below is a comprehensive overview of everything you need to prepare.

### Eligibility Requirements

- **Citizenship**: You must be a citizen of one of the seven designated English-speaking countries: USA, Canada, UK, Ireland, Australia, New Zealand, or South Africa.
- **Bachelor's Degree**: A minimum of a 4-year bachelor's degree from an accredited university in any field.
- **Clean Criminal Record**: A national-level criminal background check with no disqualifying offenses.

### Required Documents

1. **Passport** — Valid for at least 6 months beyond your intended stay
2. **Visa Application Form** (Form 34) — Available from the Korean embassy/consulate website
3. **Passport-sized Photo** — 3.5cm x 4.5cm, white background, taken within the last 6 months
4. **Original Diploma** — Your bachelor's degree, apostilled by the appropriate authority
5. **Criminal Background Check** — National-level (e.g., FBI for US citizens), apostilled
6. **Sealed Transcripts** — Official university transcripts, apostilled
7. **Contract Copy** — Signed employment contract with your Korean employer
8. **Business Registration** of the hiring institution
9. **Visa Issuance Number (VIN)** — Obtained by your employer through Korean Immigration

### Apostille vs. Embassy Legalization

Since South Korea is a member of the **Hague Apostille Convention**, documents from member countries need an **apostille** rather than embassy legalization. This simplifies the process significantly.

### Processing Timeline

| Step | Estimated Time |
|------|---------------|
| Background check | 2-16 weeks (varies by country) |
| Apostille processing | 1-4 weeks |
| VIN issuance by employer | 1-3 weeks |
| Consulate visa processing | 3-7 business days |

### Tips

- **Start your background check early** — this is almost always the longest step.
- Keep **multiple certified copies** of all documents.
- Your employer should handle the VIN application; if they ask you to do it yourself, that is a red flag.
- Some consulates now accept **mail-in applications**; check your local consulate's website.

> **Note:** Requirements can change. Always verify the latest requirements at [HiKorea](https://www.hikorea.go.kr) or your nearest Korean consulate before applying.

posts.title: Changing from E-2 to F-4 Visa (Overseas Korean Visa)
posts.body:
## E-2 to F-4 Visa Change: Guide for Eligible Teachers

The **F-4 (Overseas Korean)** visa is a long-term visa available to ethnic Koreans holding foreign citizenship. If you qualify, switching from E-2 to F-4 removes the restriction of being tied to a single employer and provides significantly more freedom.

### Who Is Eligible for F-4?

To qualify for an F-4 visa, you must meet **one** of the following criteria:

- Be a **foreign citizen of Korean descent** (gyopo) — someone who previously held Korean citizenship or whose parents/grandparents were Korean citizens
- Hold a **bachelor's degree or higher** and have Korean heritage within **two generations**
- Have specific **professional qualifications** recognized by Korea

### Key Benefits of F-4 Over E-2

| Feature | E-2 | F-4 |
|---------|-----|-----|
| **Employer restriction** | Tied to one employer | Work for any employer |
| **Duration** | 1-2 years | Up to 3 years (renewable) |
| **Job type** | English teaching only | Most jobs (some restrictions) |
| **Side jobs** | Not permitted | Generally permitted |
| **Sponsor needed** | Yes | No |

### Required Documents for Status Change

1. **Application Form** (Form 34)
2. **Passport** and **ARC**
3. **Proof of Korean heritage** — one of the following:
   - Parent's Korean family register (hojeok/gajokkwangye)
   - Parent's or grandparent's Korean passport (expired is OK)
   - Birth certificate showing Korean parent
4. **Bachelor's degree** (original, apostilled)
5. **Criminal background check** (may or may not be required; check current policy)
6. **Processing fee**: 100,000 KRW

### Process

1. **Confirm your eligibility** — The most challenging part is proving Korean heritage with official documents. Contact the Korean consulate in your home country or a **Korean immigration lawyer** for guidance.
2. **Gather heritage documentation** — This can take weeks or months if you need to request Korean family registers.
3. **Book an immigration appointment** on HiKorea.
4. **Submit your application** at your local Immigration Office.
5. **Processing time**: 2-4 weeks on average.

### Important Considerations

- **F-4 holders cannot work in certain "simple labor" fields** (manufacturing lines, agriculture, etc.), but teaching is always permitted.
- If your Korean heritage proof is through your **grandparents**, the documentation requirements are more stringent. A Korean immigration attorney (haengjeongsa) can help.
- You can apply for the **status change in-country** without leaving Korea.
- The F-4 visa is **renewable indefinitely** as long as you maintain eligibility.
- Consider whether the **F-6 (marriage)** visa might be more appropriate if you are married to a Korean citizen.

### Getting Help

For complex cases, consider consulting the **Seoul Global Center** (1688-0120) or a licensed immigration attorney. Many offer free initial consultations.

posts.title: D-10 Job Seeker Visa Guide for ESL Teachers
posts.body:
## D-10 Job Seeker Visa: Stay in Korea While Finding Your Next Position

The **D-10 (Job Seeker)** visa allows foreign professionals to remain in South Korea while searching for new employment. This is a valuable option for ESL teachers whose contracts have ended and who want to find a new position without leaving the country.

### Who Is Eligible?

- Foreign nationals who held a valid **professional work visa** (E-1 through E-7) that has expired or is about to expire
- Graduates of Korean universities with a **D-2 student visa**
- You must apply **before your current visa expires**

### Key Details

| Feature | Details |
|---------|--------|
| **Duration** | 6 months (extendable once for an additional 6 months) |
| **Work permitted** | No — job seeking activities only |
| **Part-time work** | Limited part-time work may be permitted with separate approval |
| **Maximum stay** | 1 year total |

### Required Documents

1. **Application Form** (Form 34)
2. **Passport** and **ARC**
3. **Letter of completion or termination** from your previous employer
4. **Resume/CV** in English or Korean
5. **Job search plan** — A written statement explaining your job search strategy
6. **Proof of financial means** — Bank statement showing sufficient funds to support yourself (typically 3,000,000+ KRW recommended)
7. **Previous contract** (copy)
8. **Fee**: 100,000 KRW for status change

### Application Process

1. **Prepare documents** — Gather everything listed above.
2. **Write your job search plan** — Be specific about what types of positions you are targeting, which recruiters or platforms you are using, and your timeline.
3. **Book an appointment** on HiKorea.
4. **Visit Immigration** and submit your application.
5. **Processing time**: 1-3 weeks.

### What You Can Do on a D-10

- **Attend job interviews**
- **Network** at professional events
- **Use recruitment agencies** and online job platforms
- **Take Korean language classes**

### What You Cannot Do on a D-10

- **Full-time work** of any kind
- **Freelance or contract work** (without separate permission)
- **Overstay** beyond your permitted period

### Transitioning to a New Work Visa

Once you find a new position:

1. Your new employer submits a **visa sponsorship application**
2. You apply for a **status change** from D-10 to E-2 (or whatever visa your new job requires)
3. Submit the standard documents (new contract, employer business registration, etc.)
4. Processing takes **1-3 weeks**

### Tips

- **Apply early** — Do not wait until the last day of your E-2 visa. Apply for D-10 at least **2-4 weeks** before your current visa expires.
- **Budget carefully** — You cannot work full-time on this visa, so have savings to cover 3-6 months of living expenses.
- Use platforms like **BridgeJob**, **WorknPlay**, **Dave's ESL Cafe**, and **Craigslist Korea** for job listings.
- Consider this visa as a **bridge** — it is not meant for an extended stay but rather a transition period between jobs.

posts.title: E-2 Visa Requirements
posts.body:
## E-2 Visa Requirements (Language Instructor)

The **E-2 visa** allows native English speakers to work as language instructors at registered language institutes (hagwons), public/private schools, and universities in Korea.

### Eligible Nationalities
Citizens of the following countries qualify:
- USA, Canada, UK, Ireland
- Australia, New Zealand, South Africa

### Required Documents

| Document | Notes |
|---|---|
| Valid Passport | Must be valid for 6+ months beyond visa period |
| Bachelor's Degree | Any field; must be apostilled |
| Official Transcripts | Apostilled |
| Criminal Background Check | FBI (US) or equivalent; apostilled; within 6 months |
| Medical Check | Including HIV/drug test; done at approved Korean clinic upon arrival |
| Employment Contract | Signed contract from Korean employer |
| Passport Photos | 2-4 recent photos (3.5 × 4.5 cm) |
| Application Form | DS-5540 (obtainable at Korean consulate) |

### Process Overview
1. Secure a job offer and signed contract with a Korean school
2. Gather and apostille your degree and criminal background check
3. Submit documents to the nearest Korean consulate/embassy
4. Consulate issues D-4 or E-2 visa (processing: 2-4 weeks)
5. Arrive in Korea -> complete medical exam -> register at immigration office

### Important Notes
- Apostille must be issued by the appropriate government authority in your home country
- Criminal background check must be issued within 6 months of your visa application
- Some employers sponsor the visa process; confirm with your recruiter
- Visa is initially issued for 1 year, renewable annually

For the latest requirements, always check the official Korean consulate website in your country.

posts.title: E-2 Visa Transfer — 고용 변동 시 비자 이전 절차
posts.body:
## E-2 Visa Transfer Between Employers
**고용 변동 시 비자 이전 절차**

If you are already in Korea on an E-2 visa and wish to change employers, you must complete a **visa transfer** (workplace change). This is sometimes called a "visa switch" or "sponsor change."

### When Do You Need a Transfer?

- Your current contract has ended and you are moving to a new school
- You and your employer have mutually agreed to terminate the contract early
- Your employer has closed or breached the contract

### Requirements for Transfer

**From the New Employer:**
- Business Registration Certificate (사업자등록증)
- New employment contract
- Visa transfer application form

**From the Teacher:**
- Passport and current ARC
- Letter of Release (고용변동 확인서) from your **current** employer
- Updated criminal background check — **not required** if you have maintained continuous legal residence in Korea

### The Letter of Release

This is the most critical document. Your current employer must provide a **Letter of Release** confirming they consent to the transfer.

- If your contract has ended naturally, employers are **legally required** to provide this
- If you are leaving mid-contract, the employer may be reluctant — try to negotiate
- If your employer refuses unreasonably, you can petition the immigration office directly

### Process

1. Secure a new job offer and signed contract
2. Obtain the Letter of Release from your current employer
3. Visit your local immigration office with both the new employer's documents and your release letter
4. Immigration will process the transfer (typically **1-2 weeks**)
5. Your ARC will be updated with the new employer's information

### Important Notes

- You must apply for the transfer **within 15 days** of leaving your previous employer
- Working at your new school **before** the transfer is approved is illegal
- If you leave Korea during the transfer process, you may need a new VIN to re-enter
- BRIDGE can assist with the transfer process — contact us for support
